documents.body: Computing Ethics and Society Overview This module treats ethics as part of technical practice. The focus is applied reasoning for software engineers and computer scientists who must justify design, deployment, disclosure, and governance decisions that affect people. How to use this module Read the eight concept pages in order. Page 01 defines the ethical frameworks used throughout the rest of the module. Use the cheat sheet during design reviews, risk reviews, and launch decisions. Pair the concept pages with the existing case studies and toolkit when you need templates for documentation, audits, or escalation. Concepts 01 - Ethical Frameworks for Computing 02 - Algorithmic Fairness and Bias 03 - Privacy and Data Ethics 04 - Professional Codes and Responsibilities 05 - AI Safety and Alignment Ethics 06 - Security Ethics and Responsible Disclosure 07 - Intellectual Property, Licensing, and Open Source 08 - Surveillance, Civil Liberties, and Power Cheat Sheet Ethics Cheat Sheet Case Studies Biased Hiring Model Recommender System Radicalization Health App Data Misuse GenAI Customer Support Hallucinations Toolkit Ethics Toolkit README Risk Assessment Template Model Card Template Data Card Template Incident Response Runbook Red-Team Checklist Privacy Checklist Fairness Evaluation Checklist Deployment Go/No-Go Implementation and Exercise Python implementations Ethics Review CLI

documents.title: State, Trace, and Abstraction Foundations
documents.summary: as the system state, how state evolves, what an observer can see, and what properties are checked over the resulting behavior. variables that matter for future evolution even when they are invisible in the specification. by transition systems, timed automata, LTI systems, and hybrid automata. safety, over-approximation is typically the sound direction. that is too small, so the future depends on hidden history instead of the current state.
keywords: 3. Discrete Time, Dense Time, and Continuous Evolution
documents.body: State, Trace, and Abstraction Foundations Key Ideas Every formalism in this module answers the same four questions: what counts as the system state, how state evolves, what an observer can see, and what properties are checked over the resulting behavior. State is not the same thing as observation. A model may contain internal variables that matter for future evolution even when they are invisible in the specification. Executions, traces, and reachable sets are the common semantic objects shared by transition systems, timed automata, LTI systems, and hybrid automata. Abstraction is only useful when it preserves the property of interest. For safety, over-approximation is typically the sound direction. The most common foundational modeling error is choosing a state description that is too small, so the future depends on hidden history instead of the current state. Intuition Before learning a specific formalism, it helps to know what all the formalisms have in common. Whether the model is a transition system, an LTI controller, a timed automaton, or a hybrid automaton, the core workflow is the same: define the system state, define how it changes, define what observations matter, and then ask whether the observed behavior satisfies a requirement. This is where many beginners get into trouble. They jump directly to a tool or notation and start drawing states or writing matrices before deciding what the state actually needs to remember. If the next behavior of the model depends on something that is not encoded in the state, then the model is underspecified. If the model includes many details that never affect the property, then the state space becomes harder than necessary. The right mental model is that a formalism is a particular way of packaging the same semantic ingredients. A transition system packages them as nodes and edges. An LTI model packages them as vectors and matrices. A timed automaton packages them as locations plus clocks. A hybrid automaton packages them as modes plus differential equations. The mathematics changes, but the underlying questions do not. Generic Modeling Vocabulary **Definition (State-based model).** A state-based model is a tuple M = (X, U, Step, X_0, Y, Obs) where: X is the set of states; U is the set of inputs, actions, or disturbances; Step is the evolution rule, which maps the current state and input to one or more successor states, trajectories, or timed evolutions; X_0 ⊆ X is the set of initial states; Y is the observation space; Obs : X -> Y is the observation map. Gloss: X records the information needed to predict future behavior. U captures what the environment or controller may choose. Step is the formal semantics of one modeling step. X_0 determines where runs may start. Obs tells us what part of the internal state is exposed to requirements. Constraint note: A good state description should be Markovian: once the current state and input are known, past history should not be needed to determine legal future behavior. Minimal reusable example: X = {idle_hi, move_hi, move_lo, charge_lo, charge_hi} U = {start, drain, dock, fill} X_0 = {idle_hi} Y = 2^{idle, moving, charging, low} Obs(idle_hi) = {idle}, Obs(move_lo) = {moving, low}, and so on. Executions, Traces, and Reachable Sets An **execution** is a concrete behavior generated by the evolution rule. In a discrete model it is typically a sequence of states: x_0, x_1, x_2, ... In a continuous model it may be a trajectory x(t). In a hybrid model it is an alternating sequence of continuous flows and discrete jumps. A **trace** is the corresponding observation sequence: Obs(x_0), Obs(x_1), Obs(x_2), ... for discrete models, or the observed signal over time for continuous models. The **reachable set** is the set of states that occur on some execution from an initial state. Reachability is the semantic core of many verification tasks: invariance asks whether bad states are reachable; liveness asks whether progress can be postponed forever; controller synthesis asks from which states a winning strategy exists. Discrete Time, Dense Time, and Continuous Evolution The main model families in this module differ in how Step behaves: | Formalism | State form | Evolution style | Observation style | |-----------|------------|-----------------|-------------------| | Transition system | finite symbolic state | discrete successor relation | atomic propositions on states | | Timed automaton | location plus clock valuation | delays plus action jumps | state predicates on configurations | | LTI system | real-valued vector | ODE or sampled recurrence | output map y = Cx + Du | | Hybrid automaton | mode plus real-valued state | continuous flow plus guarded jumps | predicates on mode-state pairs | This difference changes the analysis technique, but not the basic semantics: all four produce executions, traces, and reachable sets. Abstraction and Property Preservation An abstraction map alpha : X_C -> X_A compresses a concrete state space into a smaller abstract one. The abstract model is an **over-approximation** when every concrete execution maps to an abstract execution, possibly along with extra abstract behaviors not present concretely. **Theorem (Trace equivalence preserves trace properties).** Let M_1 and M_2 be two models over the same observation space. If they induce exactly the same set of traces, then for every trace property P, M_1 ⊨ P iff M_2 ⊨ P. Plain-English reading: if two models generate the same observable behaviors, then no trace-based specification can distinguish them. *Proof sketch.* A trace property is just a predicate on traces. If Traces(M_1) = Traces(M_2), then every trace accepted or rejected by P in one model is accepted or rejected in the other. Therefore the universal statement "all traces satisfy P" has the same truth value in both models. This is the basic semantic reason that abstraction, quotienting, and equivalence checking matter in verification. The theorem is intentionally simple, but it gives the right engineering rule: if you abstract away information, make sure the abstraction preserves the observable behaviors relevant to the property you plan to check. Worked Example: Rover Battery Controller Consider a simplified rover controller with full state set: idle_hi: rover waiting with healthy battery move_hi: rover moving with healthy battery move_lo: rover moving with low battery charge_lo: rover docked and charging from low battery charge_hi: rover docked and charging with restored battery Actions: start: idle_hi -> move_hi drain: move_hi -> move_lo dock: move_lo -> charge_lo fill: charge_lo -> charge_hi stop: charge_hi -> idle_hi Observation map: Obs(idle_hi) = {idle} Obs(move_hi) = {moving} Obs(move_lo) = {moving, low} Obs(charge_lo) = {charging, low} Obs(charge_hi) = {charging} Initial state: X_0 = {idle_hi}. 5.1 Executable trace | Step | Full state | Observation | Action taken | Next state | |------|------------|-------------|--------------|------------| | 0 | idle_hi | {idle} | start | move_hi | | 1 | move_hi | {moving} | drain | move_lo | | 2 | move_lo | {moving, low} | dock | charge_lo | | 3 | charge_lo | {charging, low} | fill | charge_hi | | 4 | charge_hi | {charging} | stop | idle_hi | 5.2 Property check The safety property is: G not (moving and charging). The trace satisfies it because no observation contains both moving and charging. The example also shows why state and observation are different: move_hi and move_lo are different internal states because future actions differ; both are still observations of motion from the perspective of a high-level safety property. 5.3 Abstracted model If the property only mentions moving, charging, and low, then the exact battery charge value is irrelevant. We can abstract the state space to: idle move_ok move_low charge_low charge_ok This abstraction preserves the worked-example safety property because it preserves the same observation traces. Where This Page Connects 02-unified-modeling-overview.md places the module's formalisms in one lattice. 03-sequential-circuits-and-transition-systems.md specializes the generic vocabulary to finite-state discrete models. 06-lti-systems-continuous-discrete.md, 07-timed-automata-basics.md, and 08-hybrid-systems-intuition.md each instantiate the same state-trace viewpoint with different evolution laws. Common Mistakes Treating visible outputs as if they were the whole state, even when hidden variables affect future behavior. Choosing a state that depends on unrecorded history, which makes the model non-Markovian. Abstracting away timing or continuous variables before checking whether the property depends on them. Confusing an execution with a trace; many different executions may induce the same observation trace. Claiming an abstraction is sound without stating what property class it preserves. Practical Checklist [ ] Write down the state variables before choosing a formalism. [ ] Ask whether the chosen state is sufficient to predict all legal futures given the current input. [ ] Separate internal state from observable labels or outputs. [ ] Define what one step of evolution means: successor, delay, flow, or jump. [ ] Compute at least one concrete execution and its induced observation trace. [ ] State explicitly which properties the abstraction is meant to preserve. References Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Clarke, Edmund M., Orna Grumberg, and Doron Peled. 1999. *Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262032704/model-checking/> Henzinger, Thomas A., and Joseph Sifakis. 2006. The Embedded Systems Design Challenge. *FM 2006*. <https://doi.org/10.1007/11813040_1> Lee, Edward A., and Sanjit A. Seshia. 2017. *Introduction to Embedded Systems*. 2nd ed. <https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/books/leeseshia/> Lynch, Nancy. 1996. *Distributed Algorithms*. Morgan Kaufmann. Pnueli, Amir. 1977. The Temporal Logic of Programs. *FOCS 1977*: 46-57. <https://doi.org/10.1109/SFCS.1977.32>

documents.title: Unified Modeling Overview
documents.summary: Autonomous systems sit at the boundary between software and physics. A robot, vehicle, or medical device does not merely execute instructions once; it keeps interacting with an environment while its internal state, clocks, and physical variables evolve over time. That means there is no single mathematical model that captures everything conveniently.
keywords: 5. Worked Example: One System, Four Abstractions
documents.body: Unified Modeling Overview Key Ideas No single formalism is right for every autonomous-system problem; the choice is a three-way trade-off between expressiveness, decidability, and tool support. The module's formalisms form an abstraction lattice: transition systems forget time and continuous state, timed automata add dense clocks, and hybrid automata add mode-dependent continuous dynamics. Expressiveness usually increases faster than algorithmic tractability: finite transition systems admit exhaustive search, timed automata retain decidable reachability, and general hybrid automata do not. Sound abstraction depends on the property of interest: over-approximations are sound for proving safety, while under-approximations are sound only for finding bugs. Verification is a pipeline, not a button press. Errors can enter at the requirements, formalization, modeling, algorithm, or interpretation stage. Intuition Autonomous systems sit at the boundary between software and physics. A robot, vehicle, or medical device does not merely execute instructions once; it keeps interacting with an environment while its internal state, clocks, and physical variables evolve over time. That means there is no single mathematical model that captures everything conveniently. The practical question is therefore not "what is the most realistic model?" but "what is the simplest model that preserves the property I care about?" If the property is a pure sequencing rule such as "never open both valves at once," a finite transition system may be enough. If the rule is time-bounded, such as "send an acknowledgement within 100 ms," clocks must be modeled explicitly. If the plant's velocity, temperature, or voltage matters, continuous dynamics enter the picture and a control-theoretic or hybrid model becomes necessary. This page is the map for the rest of the module. It tells you what each formalism can represent, what it deliberately ignores, and what that buys you algorithmically. The aim is not to memorize a taxonomy but to learn how to move between levels of abstraction without invalidating the conclusion you want to draw. Formalism Lattices 1.1 System-model lattice Moving downward adds modeling power: Finite automata capture finite-state control flow. Transition systems add state labels and general transition structure. Timed automata add dense-time clocks. Hybrid automata add real-valued state with continuous evolution. General dynamical systems drop the finite-control restrictions entirely. 1.2 Logic lattice The logic lattice matters because a model and a property language must match. LTL is interpreted over single paths, while CTL and CTL* reason over branching trees of paths. This module uses LTL as the default temporal logic and mentions TCTL for timed-model queries in tools such as UPPAAL. Comparison Table | Formalism | State space | Time model | Decidable reachability? | Primary tool | Primary use | |-----------|-------------|------------|--------------------------|--------------|-------------| | Finite automaton | Finite discrete | None | Yes, linear in graph size | Lexer / parser generators | Parsing, simple protocols | | Labelled transition system | Finite discrete | Implicit discrete steps | Yes, O(|S| + |→|) | SPIN, NuSMV | Software and controller logic | | Timed automaton | Loc x R^n | Dense real time | Yes, PSPACE-complete | UPPAAL, KRONOS | Real-time protocols and schedulers | | Initialised rectangular hybrid automaton | Finite modes x R^n | Dense real time | Yes | PHAVer | Restricted cyber-physical safety | | Linear hybrid automaton | Finite modes x R^n | Dense real time | Undecidable in general | SpaceEx (approximate) | CPS reachability over-approximation | | LTI system | R^n | Continuous or sampled | Stability decidable; arbitrary specs need more structure | MATLAB, Python control, JuliaControl | Control design and analysis | The pattern is consistent: each row adds modeling detail, but only some rows retain complete decision procedures. When a tool appears to "verify" an undecidable class, read the guarantee carefully. It is usually computing an over-approximation, a bounded result, or an incomplete search. Abstraction and Refinement An **abstraction** discards distinctions that are irrelevant to a property. A continuous vehicle model may abstract to modes {cruise, brake, stop} if the only question is whether stopping eventually occurs. A **refinement** adds detail back in when the abstract model is too coarse to decide the property or produces a spurious counterexample. Formally, let C be a concrete transition system and A an abstract one. An abstraction map alpha : S_C -> S_A groups concrete states into abstract states. The abstraction is an **over-approximation** if every concrete transition maps to an abstract transition, possibly along with extra abstract transitions that do not correspond to any concrete behavior. **Proposition (Safety preservation under over-approximation).** Let C be a concrete model and A an over-approximation of C. If every path of A satisfies the safety property G safe, then every path of C satisfies G safe. *Proof sketch.* Safety properties are violated by finite bad prefixes. If C violated G safe, there would be a concrete bad prefix. Because A over-approximates C, the abstract image of that prefix would also be a path of A, contradicting A ⊨ G safe. The argument fails for liveness because an over-approximation can add unfair or non-progressing behaviors. Full proofs of abstraction soundness appear in standard model-checking texts such as Baier and Katoen. The dual warning is important: an under-approximation may be useful for bug hunting, but proving safety on an under-approximation proves almost nothing about the original system. Verification Pipeline | Stage | Question | Typical artifact | Common failure mode | |-------|----------|------------------|---------------------| | Requirements | What must the system do or avoid? | Structured English requirement | Ambiguous or conflicting requirement | | Formal specification | How is the requirement written mathematically? | G safe, F goal, TCTL query | Wrong logic or vacuous formula | | Model construction | What state variables and dynamics are kept? | TS, TA, hybrid automaton, LTI model | Abstraction drops the critical behavior | | Verification | What algorithm or tool is appropriate? | BFS, Büchi emptiness, zones, SMT | Using an incomplete or bounded method as if complete | | Interpretation | What does the result actually prove? | Counterexample trace or proof obligation | Confusing model correctness with system correctness | The pipeline is iterative. Counterexamples often reveal a requirement bug or a modeling omission rather than a defect in the real system. Formal methods are most effective when verification and model refinement are treated as a loop. Worked Example: One System, Four Abstractions Consider an obstacle-response controller for a mobile robot: The concrete plant tracks speed v(t) continuously. On obstacle detection, the controller must enter braking within 0.5 s. Once braking starts, the robot must stop. We can view the same behavior at four levels: | Formalism | State description | What it keeps | What it omits | |-----------|-------------------|---------------|---------------| | LTI | v(t) with dv/dt = -2v in braking mode | Speed profile | Mode-switch logic | | Hybrid automaton | (mode, v) with modes cruise, brake, stop | Continuous deceleration and switching | Low-level software states | | Timed automaton | (location, x) where clock x measures time since obstacle | The 0.5 s deadline | Continuous speed magnitude | | Transition system | {cruise, brake, stop} | Ordering of modes | Real time and real-valued speed | 5.1 Concrete trace Assume the obstacle is detected at t = 0, initial speed is v(0) = 4 m/s, and the controller switches to brake at t = 0.3 s. | Step | Time / mode | State | Transition | Conclusion | |------|-------------|-------|------------|------------| | 0 | t = 0, cruise | v = 4 | obstacle detected | deadline clock starts | | 1 | t = 0.3, brake | v = 4 | discrete mode switch | deadline met (0.3 <= 0.5) | | 2 | t = 1.0, brake | v = 4e^{-2(0.7)} ≈ 0.99 | continuous evolution | slowing but not yet stopped | | 3 | t = 3.0, stop abstraction | v ≈ 0.018 | thresholded to stop | effectively stopped | 5.2 Property check The timed requirement is "within 0.5 s of obstacle detection, braking must occur." A transition system cannot even state this because it has no metric time. A timed automaton can express it with a clock guard; a hybrid automaton can express both the deadline and the speed trajectory; an LTI model can analyze the braking dynamics but not the discrete switching policy by itself. The trace therefore demonstrates the central modeling lesson of the module: choose the weakest formalism that still expresses the property of interest. Common Mistakes Treating higher expressiveness as automatically better. It often just makes the verification problem harder without adding information relevant to the requirement. Using a time-free transition system for a deadline property, then claiming the deadline has been verified. Proving safety on an under-approximate model and presenting the result as a system-level guarantee. Ignoring the distinction between "tool says reachable" and "property fails in the physical system." The counterexample may exploit an abstraction artifact. Moving between LTL and CTL notation as if they were interchangeable. Practical Checklist [ ] Identify whether the system is purely discrete, real-time, continuous, or genuinely hybrid before choosing a model. [ ] State the property first; model choice should follow the property, not the other way around. [ ] Record which behaviors the abstraction deliberately omits and why they are irrelevant to the current property. [ ] Use over-approximations for safety proofs and be explicit when a result is bounded or approximate. [ ] Validate the model against at least one known-correct and one known-incorrect trace before trusting tool output. [ ] Check whether the logic used by the tool matches the logic of the requirement. References Alur, Rajeev, and David L. Dill. 1994. A Theory of Timed Automata. *Theoretical Computer Science* 126 (2): 183-235. <https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-3975(94)90010-8> Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Clarke, Edmund M., E. Allen Emerson, and A. Prasad Sistla. 1986. Automatic Verification of Finite-State Concurrent Systems Using Temporal Logic Specifications. *ACM TOPLAS* 8 (2): 244-263. <https://doi.org/10.1145/5397.5399> Henzinger, Thomas A., Peter W. Kopke, Anuj Puri, and Pravin Varaiya. 1995. What's Decidable about Hybrid Automata? *STOC 1995*. <https://doi.org/10.1145/225058.225162> Lee, Edward A., and Sanjit A. Seshia. 2017. *Introduction to Embedded Systems*. 2nd ed. <https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/books/leeseshia/> Pnueli, Amir. 1977. The Temporal Logic of Programs. *FOCS 1977*: 46-57. <https://doi.org/10.1109/SFCS.1977.32>

documents.title: Sequential Circuits and Transition Systems
documents.summary: module: it captures possible behaviors as paths but intentionally omits metric time, probability, and real-valued dynamics. checking; LTL is interpreted over their infinite paths. breadth-first or depth-first search runs in `O(|S| + |→|)`. often due to concurrency or underspecification. transition system with at most `2^n` states, which is why state explosion appears so quickly in hardware-style models.
documents.body: Sequential Circuits and Transition Systems Key Ideas A labelled transition system (LTS) is the foundational discrete model for the module: it captures possible behaviors as paths but intentionally omits metric time, probability, and real-valued dynamics. Kripke structures are the state-labelled variant used most often in model checking; LTL is interpreted over their infinite paths. Reachability in a finite transition system is algorithmically simple: breadth-first or depth-first search runs in O(|S| + |→|). Non-determinism is not probability. It models multiple possible successors, often due to concurrency or underspecification. A synchronous sequential circuit with n bits of memory induces a finite transition system with at most 2^n states, which is why state explosion appears so quickly in hardware-style models. Intuition A transition system is the simplest serious model of computation for reactive systems. Instead of asking what output a program produces and then halting, it asks what states a system can move through forever. That matches how controllers, protocols, schedulers, and digital circuits actually behave: they do not return one answer and stop, they continue interacting with inputs. The model is deliberately austere. A state records whatever information is needed to predict the next step. A transition says the system may move from one state to another, optionally with an action label that explains why. There is no stopwatch, no differential equation, and no probability distribution. Those omissions are not defects. They are what make exhaustive graph search possible. Sequential circuits fit naturally into this view. At each clock tick, the combinational logic computes the next values of the flip-flops from the current values and inputs. If you treat the vector of stored bits as the state, the circuit becomes a finite transition system. Model checking then reduces to graph exploration over those states. Formal Definitions **Definition (Labelled Transition System).** A labelled transition system is a tuple TS = (S, Act, →, I, AP, L) where: S is a finite or countable set of states; Act is a set of actions; → ⊆ S × Act × S is the transition relation; I ⊆ S is the set of initial states; AP is a set of atomic propositions; L : S -> 2^AP is the state-labelling function. Informal gloss: S says what configurations the system can be in. Act names the externally visible or modeling-relevant steps. → describes which next states are possible. I tells us where executions may start. AP provides the vocabulary used in temporal formulas. L tells us which propositions hold in each state. Constraints not captured by the type: Usually I != ∅; otherwise the model has no executions. For reactive systems, deadlock states are often undesirable even though the definition permits them. Minimal reusable example: S = {red_NS, green_NS, yellow_NS, green_EW, yellow_EW} Act = {tick} I = {red_NS} AP = {green_NS, yellow_NS, green_EW, yellow_EW} L(red_NS) = ∅, L(green_NS) = {green_NS}, and so on. **Definition (Kripke Structure).** A Kripke structure is a tuple K = (S, I, R, AP, L) where: S is a set of states; I ⊆ S is the set of initial states; R ⊆ S × S is the transition relation; AP is a set of atomic propositions; L : S -> 2^AP is the state-labelling function. Informal gloss: A Kripke structure drops explicit action labels. It is the standard input model for LTL and CTL model checking. Constraint note: Many textbooks require R to be total so that every path can be extended to an infinite path. If a model contains deadlocks, one common repair is to add a self-loop at each deadlock state before interpreting LTL. Paths, Traces, Reachability, and Deadlock A **path** in TS is a sequence π = s_0, s_1, s_2, ... such that for every i >= 0 there exists an action a_i with s_i --a_i--> s_{i+1}. A path is **finite** if it ends in a state with no chosen successor and **infinite** otherwise. The notation π[i] denotes the ith state, and π^i the suffix starting at position i. A state t is **reachable** if there exists a finite path from some s_0 ∈ I to t. The set of reachable states is: Reach(TS) = { t ∈ S | exists s_0 ∈ I and path s_0 ->* t }. A state s is a **deadlock** if it has no outgoing transition: deadlock(s) iff not exists a ∈ Act, s' ∈ S : s --a--> s'. Deadlock matters because a reactive controller that deadlocks has stopped responding to its environment, even if no explicit safety property has yet been violated. **Theorem (Graph search decides reachability).** For a finite transition system TS = (S, Act, →, I, AP, L), reachability of a target set T ⊆ S is decidable by BFS or DFS in time O(|S| + |→|). *Proof sketch.* Treat the transition system as a directed graph whose vertices are states and whose edges are the pairs (s, s') for which some action yields s --a--> s'. BFS and DFS each visit every reachable vertex at most once and inspect every outgoing edge at most once, hence the linear bound. Soundness is immediate because any discovered target state lies on a concrete path from an initial state; completeness follows because every reachable state is eventually explored. Full proof details appear in standard graph algorithm texts and in Baier and Katoen's model-checking treatment. Determinism and Non-Determinism An LTS is **deterministic** if, for every state s and action a, there is at most one successor s' such that s --a--> s'. It is **non-deterministic** when multiple successors are possible. Non-determinism is useful for two distinct reasons: **Concurrency.** Different schedulings of concurrent components induce different successors. **Underspecification.** The model leaves implementation choices open. The same mathematics covers both cases, but the interpretation differs. In a correctness proof, a non-deterministic environment is usually adversarial: the property must hold for every possible successor, not just a likely one. Sequential Circuits as Transition Systems A synchronous sequential circuit has: a vector q ∈ {0,1}^n of stored bits; an input vector u ∈ {0,1}^m; a next-state function f : {0,1}^n × {0,1}^m -> {0,1}^n; an output function g. At each clock tick, the circuit updates q to f(q, u). This induces an LTS: states are the bit vectors q; actions can be the input valuations u; transitions are q --u--> f(q, u); initial states are reset configurations. If the circuit has n state bits, then the induced state space has at most 2^n states. This simple counting fact explains why even small synchronous controllers can become difficult to explore exhaustively. Worked Example: Traffic-Light Controller We model a four-phase traffic light that alternates between north-south and east-west traffic. The safety requirement is that both directions are never green simultaneously. **Definition (Traffic-light transition system).** Let TS_TL = (S, Act, →, I, AP, L) where: S = {NS_G, NS_Y, ALL_R_1, EW_G, EW_Y, ALL_R_2}; Act = {tick}; I = {ALL_R_1}; AP = {green_NS, yellow_NS, green_EW, yellow_EW}; L(ALL_R_1) = ∅, L(NS_G) = {green_NS}, L(NS_Y) = {yellow_NS}, L(EW_G) = {green_EW}, L(EW_Y) = {yellow_EW}, L(ALL_R_2) = ∅; → contains exactly the transitions: ALL_R_1 --tick--> NS_G, NS_G --tick--> NS_Y, NS_Y --tick--> ALL_R_2, ALL_R_2 --tick--> EW_G, EW_G --tick--> EW_Y, EW_Y --tick--> ALL_R_1. All six states are reachable from the initial state, because the controller is a single directed cycle. 5.1 Executable trace | Step | State | Enabled actions | Action taken | Next state | |------|-------|-----------------|--------------|------------| | 0 | ALL_R_1 | {tick} | tick | NS_G | | 1 | NS_G | {tick} | tick | NS_Y | | 2 | NS_Y | {tick} | tick | ALL_R_2 | | 3 | ALL_R_2 | {tick} | tick | EW_G | | 4 | EW_G | {tick} | tick | EW_Y | | 5 | EW_Y | {tick} | tick | ALL_R_1 | 5.2 Property check The safety property is: TS_TL ⊨ G ¬(green_NS ∧ green_EW). Inspection of the labelling function shows that no state carries both green_NS and green_EW. Because every reachable state is one of the six listed above, the bad condition is unreachable. Equivalently, if we define bad = green_NS ∧ green_EW, then Reach(TS_TL) contains no state labelled with bad. The example also illustrates deadlock freedom: every state has exactly one outgoing transition, so every maximal path is infinite. Connection to Other Formalisms LTL formulas are interpreted over the infinite paths of Kripke structures; see 04-ltl-basics-trace-semantics.md. Timed automata extend transition systems with real-valued clocks and delay transitions; see 07-timed-automata-basics.md. A sequential circuit is often the finite-state controller inside a larger hybrid or continuous plant model. Common Mistakes Omitting state variables that are needed to predict the future, which makes the model non-Markovian. Treating a deadlock as harmless because "the bad state was never reached." A reactive controller that stops responding is usually itself a fault. Reading non-determinism as probability. A possible bad branch is enough to falsify universal correctness. Forgetting to totalize a Kripke structure before interpreting LTL on it. Using reachability on the full syntactic state space rather than the reachable subgraph. Practical Checklist [ ] Write the full tuple TS = (S, Act, →, I, AP, L) before stating any property. [ ] Confirm that each atomic proposition in the property appears in AP and is labelled by L. [ ] Enumerate or algorithmically compute the reachable states, not just the syntactic state set. [ ] Check explicitly for deadlocks. [ ] Decide whether non-determinism represents concurrency or underspecification. [ ] If the model comes from a circuit, ensure the reset behavior is included in I. References Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Clarke, Edmund M., Orna Grumberg, and Doron Peled. 1999. *Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262032704/model-checking/> Huth, Michael, and Mark Ryan. 2004. *Logic in Computer Science*. 2nd ed. Cambridge University Press. Holzmann, Gerard J. 2003. *The SPIN Model Checker*. Addison-Wesley. <https://spinroot.com/spin/Doc/Book_extras/> Lee, Edward A., and Sanjit A. Seshia. 2017. *Introduction to Embedded Systems*. 2nd ed. <https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/books/leeseshia/> Wakerly, John F. 2005. *Digital Design: Principles and Practices*. 4th ed. Pearson.

documents.title: LTL Basics and Trace Semantics
documents.summary: models ordering and recurrence over time, but it does not directly quantify over branching alternatives the way CTL does. glosses like "always" and "eventually" are only shorthand for that definition. satisfies `φ` exactly when the product with a Büchi automaton for `¬φ` has no accepting run. of the transition system; the exponential blow-up comes from the property, not from graph search on the model. syntactically correct yet prove almost nothing about the intended system.
documents.body: LTL Basics and Trace Semantics Key Ideas Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) specifies properties of single infinite paths; it models ordering and recurrence over time, but it does not directly quantify over branching alternatives the way CTL does. The formal semantics is an inductive definition of π, i ⊨ φ; informal glosses like "always" and "eventually" are only shorthand for that definition. LTL model checking reduces to automata emptiness: a transition system satisfies φ exactly when the product with a Büchi automaton for ¬φ has no accepting run. The complexity is PSPACE-complete in the formula size and linear in the size of the transition system; the exponential blow-up comes from the property, not from graph search on the model. Vacuity and mismatched assumptions are common failure modes: a formula may be syntactically correct yet prove almost nothing about the intended system. Intuition Reactive systems do not just need state predicates such as "the door is open" or "the alarm is off." They need temporal promises: if a request is issued, a grant eventually follows; once the system enters an emergency mode, it stays there until reset; a sensor heartbeat occurs infinitely often. LTL was invented to write those promises over executions of a system. LTL takes the viewpoint of a single run through time. You imagine one infinite path and ask whether the formula is true along that path. This is why the logic is called linear-time: it reasons about one timeline at a time. A model checker then lifts that path-level notion to a system-level question by asking whether all paths of the model satisfy the formula. The power of LTL comes from a small set of temporal operators with precise semantics. The danger is that they are easy to read too casually. "Eventually" does not mean "soon," and "always eventually" is very different from "eventually always." The only safe way to reason with LTL is to return to the formal satisfaction relation. Syntax LTL formulas are generated by: where p ranges over atomic propositions in AP. **Definition (LTL formula).** An LTL formula is any expression generated by the grammar above. Gloss: X φ: φ holds at the next position. F φ: φ holds at some future position. G φ: φ holds at every future position. φ U ψ: φ holds until ψ holds, and ψ must eventually hold. φ W ψ: weak until; ψ need not ever hold. φ R ψ: release; ψ must hold until and including the point where φ becomes true, if that ever happens. Semantics over Infinite Paths Let TS = (S, Act, →, I, AP, L) be a transition system. Let π = s_0, s_1, s_2, ... be an infinite path. The satisfaction relation π, i ⊨ φ is defined inductively. **Definition (LTL trace semantics).** For every infinite path π and position i >= 0: π, i ⊨ true always; π, i ⊭ false always; π, i ⊨ p iff p ∈ L(π[i]); π, i ⊨ ¬φ iff not π, i ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ φ ∧ ψ iff π, i ⊨ φ and π, i ⊨ ψ; π, i ⊨ φ ∨ ψ iff π, i ⊨ φ or π, i ⊨ ψ; π, i ⊨ φ → ψ iff π, i ⊭ φ or π, i ⊨ ψ; π, i ⊨ X φ iff π, i + 1 ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ F φ iff there exists j >= i such that π, j ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ G φ iff for all j >= i, π, j ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ φ U ψ iff there exists j >= i such that π, j ⊨ ψ and for all k with i <= k < j, π, k ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ φ W ψ iff either π, i ⊨ φ U ψ or for all j >= i, π, j ⊨ φ; π, i ⊨ φ R ψ iff for all j >= i, if for all k with i <= k < j, π, k ⊭ φ, then π, j ⊨ ψ. We write π ⊨ φ as shorthand for π, 0 ⊨ φ. A transition system satisfies φ, written TS ⊨ φ, if every infinite path starting from an initial state satisfies φ. Derived Equivalences **Proposition (Standard LTL equivalences).** For every path π and position i: F φ ≡ true U φ G φ ≡ false R φ φ W ψ ≡ (φ U ψ) ∨ G φ G F φ ≡ ¬F G ¬φ *Proof sketch.* Each equivalence follows by expanding the semantic definitions. For example, π, i ⊨ true U φ means there exists j >= i such that π, j ⊨ φ and true holds at all earlier positions, which is exactly the definition of F φ. The release equivalence is analogous. The duality G F φ ≡ ¬F G ¬φ is a De Morgan law over temporal quantification: "infinitely often φ" is equivalent to "it is not the case that eventually ¬φ holds forever." Full inductive proofs are standard in Pnueli 1977 and later texts. The LTL Model-Checking Problem The decision problem is: Given a finite transition system TS and an LTL formula φ, decide whether TS ⊨ φ. The automata-theoretic solution proceeds in four steps: Translate ¬φ into a Büchi automaton A_{¬φ}. Compute the synchronous product TS ⊗ A_{¬φ}. Check whether the product has an accepting run, equivalently a reachable cycle containing an accepting state. If such a cycle exists, project it to a counterexample path of TS; if not, then TS ⊨ φ. **Theorem (Automata-theoretic characterization).** Let TS be a finite transition system and φ an LTL formula. Then TS ⊨ φ iff the product TS ⊗ A_{¬φ} has no accepting run. Plain-English reading: the system satisfies the property exactly when no system execution is accepted by the automaton representing the bad behaviors. *Proof sketch.* The Büchi automaton A_{¬φ} is constructed so that it accepts exactly the traces satisfying ¬φ. The product runs the system and the property automaton in lockstep, so an accepting run of the product is precisely a path of TS whose label sequence violates φ. Conversely, any violating path induces an accepting run in the product. Full proof in standard references such as Vardi and Wolper's automata-theoretic treatment and Baier and Katoen. The complexity of LTL model checking is PSPACE-complete in |φ| and linear in the size of TS. Worked Example: G (req -> F grant) Consider the transition system TS_req with states: s_0: idle, L(s_0) = ∅ s_1: request pending, L(s_1) = {req} s_2: granted, L(s_2) = {grant} s_3: starvation loop, L(s_3) = {req} Transitions: s_0 -> s_1 s_1 -> s_2 s_2 -> s_0 s_1 -> s_3 s_3 -> s_3 The property is φ = G (req -> F grant). 5.1 A Büchi automaton for ¬φ Using the equivalence ¬G(req -> F grant) ≡ F(req ∧ G ¬grant), we can build a Büchi automaton with states: q_0: no pending violation chosen yet q_1: a request has been seen; waiting to confirm perpetual lack of grant q_acc: accepting state meaning "from here on, no grant occurs" q_dead: grant occurred after the bad guess Representative transitions: from q_0, stay in q_0 on labels without req; move to q_1 on labels containing req; from q_1, move to q_0 if grant occurs; stay in q_1 if not; and nondeterministically move to q_acc when we guess the current suffix has no future grant; from q_acc, stay in q_acc on labels without grant; move to q_dead if grant appears; q_dead is a rejecting sink. Only q_acc is accepting. 5.2 Product search The bad branch of the system is: s_0, s_1, s_3, s_3, s_3, ... In the product, this yields the reachable accepting cycle: (s_0, q_0) -> (s_1, q_1) -> (s_3, q_acc) -> (s_3, q_acc) -> ... because s_3 is labelled with req but not grant, so the Büchi automaton can remain in its accepting state forever. 5.3 Executable trace and property check | Step | TS state | Labels | Büchi state | Reason | |------|----------|--------|-------------|--------| | 0 | s_0 | ∅ | q_0 | no request yet | | 1 | s_1 | {req} | q_1 | request creates an obligation | | 2 | s_3 | {req} | q_acc | guess that grant will never occur | | 3 | s_3 | {req} | q_acc | still no grant | | 4 | s_3 | {req} | q_acc | accepting cycle | Since the product has an accepting run, TS_req ⊭ G (req -> F grant). Specification Pitfalls **Vacuous satisfaction.** If req is never true, then G(req -> F grant) holds vacuously. **Unrealizable liveness.** A formula may require progress that the model's environment prevents. **Finite-trace confusion.** Standard LTL is defined over infinite paths. Tool support for finite traces uses separate semantics. A good practice is to test a specification against a known-good trace and a known-bad trace before running the full model checker. Common Mistakes Explaining G or F informally without giving the actual π, i ⊨ φ semantics. Mixing LTL with CTL notation, such as writing AG p in a page that is otherwise about LTL. Confusing F G p with G F p; one means eventual stabilization, the other means infinitely often. Interpreting "eventually" as a bounded deadline. Plain LTL has no metric time. Accepting a vacuous proof without checking whether the antecedent ever holds. Practical Checklist [ ] Define the atomic propositions and the path model before writing formulas. [ ] State formulas in standard LTL syntax using X, F, G, U, W, and R. [ ] Test the formula on one intended-satisfying and one intended-violating trace. [ ] Check for vacuity, especially in implication-heavy specifications. [ ] Use the automata-theoretic view to interpret a model checker's counterexample as an accepting cycle. [ ] Be explicit when working with finite-trace semantics instead of standard infinite-trace LTL. References Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Clarke, Edmund M., Orna Grumberg, and Doron Peled. 1999. *Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262032704/model-checking/> Pnueli, Amir. 1977. The Temporal Logic of Programs. *FOCS 1977*: 46-57. <https://doi.org/10.1109/SFCS.1977.32> Vardi, Moshe Y., and Pierre Wolper. 1986. An Automata-Theoretic Approach to Automatic Program Verification. *LICS 1986*. <https://doi.org/10.1109/LICS.1986.2213> Wolper, Pierre. 1983. Temporal Logic Can Be More Expressive. *Information and Control* 56 (1-2): 72-99. <https://doi.org/10.1016/S0019-9958(83)80051-5> Holzmann, Gerard J. 2003. *The SPIN Model Checker*. Addison-Wesley. <https://spinroot.com/spin/Doc/Book_extras/>

documents.title: Requirements: Safety, Liveness, Reachability
documents.summary: reachability, while liveness requires reasoning about infinite behaviors and accepting cycles. interchangeable. A system can reach a goal once without guaranteeing eventual service on all paths. liveness part; this is a structural theorem, not a slogan. them changes the claim being made about the system. the English statement was underspecified or vacuous.
keywords: 2. Safety, Liveness, and the Alpern-Schneider Decomposition
documents.body: Requirements: Safety, Liveness, Reachability Key Ideas Requirement class determines verification method: safety reduces to bad-state reachability, while liveness requires reasoning about infinite behaviors and accepting cycles. Reachability, invariance, persistence, and response are related but not interchangeable. A system can reach a goal once without guaranteeing eventual service on all paths. Every omega-regular property can be decomposed into a safety part and a liveness part; this is a structural theorem, not a slogan. Fairness assumptions are sometimes necessary for liveness proofs, but adding them changes the claim being made about the system. Many real requirements fail not because the logic is too weak, but because the English statement was underspecified or vacuous. Intuition When engineers say "the system is correct," they usually mean several different things at once. Some requirements say that something bad must never happen: two trains must never occupy the same block, both lanes must never show green, the pacemaker must never pace faster than its safety limit. Other requirements say that something good must eventually happen: a request is eventually served, the elevator eventually reaches the requested floor, the robot eventually docks. Those are not the same kind of claim. A safety violation has a finite witness: once the bad thing happens, the requirement is already broken. A liveness violation has no finite witness, because any finite execution prefix can still be extended by doing the right thing later. That difference is why model checkers use different algorithms for the two cases. Formal specification patterns help translate English requirements into LTL without reinventing the logic every time. They do not remove the need for judgment, but they do prevent many common mistakes, especially in recurring structures such as response, absence, and precedence. Formal Definitions A property P ⊆ (2^AP)^ω is a set of infinite traces. **Definition (Safety property).** A property P is a safety property iff every violating trace has a finite bad prefix: for every σ ∉ P, there exists a finite prefix u of σ such that every infinite extension of u is also outside P. Gloss: once a safety property is violated, the violation can be detected after a finite amount of behavior. **Definition (Liveness property).** A property P is a liveness property iff every finite trace prefix can be extended to some infinite trace in P. Gloss: no finite observation is enough to refute liveness. **Definition (Reachability and invariance).** For a transition system TS and state predicates goal and safe: reachability is expressed by F goal; invariance is expressed by G safe. Their duality is immediate: TS ⊨ G ¬bad iff no reachable state satisfies bad. Safety, Liveness, and the Alpern-Schneider Decomposition Let cl(P) denote the topological closure of P in the prefix topology over infinite traces. **Theorem (Alpern-Schneider decomposition).** Every omega-regular property P can be written as P = S ∩ L where S is a safety property and L is a liveness property. Plain-English reading: every temporal requirement has a "nothing bad ever happens" part and a "something good eventually happens" part. *Proof sketch.* Take S = cl(P), the closure of P. This is a safety property because closure exactly captures the idea that violating traces expose a finite bad prefix. Then define L = P ∪ complement(cl(P)). This L is liveness because any finite prefix can be extended either into P or into a trace that escapes the closure of P; intuitively, liveness is the dense component left after safety has captured all finitely refutable behavior. By construction, P = cl(P) ∩ (P ∪ complement(cl(P))) = S ∩ L. Full proof in Alpern and Schneider 1985. Verification Consequences For safety, verification reduces to bad-state reachability: define bad as the undesirable predicate; search for a reachable state satisfying bad; if one exists, the path to it is a counterexample. For liveness, reachability is not enough. A liveness counterexample is an infinite path that fails to make progress, which is why Büchi acceptance or fair-cycle detection is needed. Canonical LTL forms: safety: G ¬bad reachability: F goal recurrence / liveness: G F p persistence: F G p response: G (p -> F q) Fairness Fairness constrains which infinite paths count as admissible behaviors. **Weak fairness (justice):** if an action is continuously enabled from some point onward, it must eventually occur. **Strong fairness (compassion):** if an action is enabled infinitely often, it must eventually occur. Fairness matters because many liveness claims are false in the raw transition system but true under an assumption that the scheduler or environment will not starve a continuously enabled component forever. You should state fairness explicitly. Adding fairness changes the theorem from "the system guarantees liveness" to "the system guarantees liveness under the fairness assumption." Specification Patterns Catalogue The table below uses: p as the main triggering proposition; s as the response or constrained proposition; t as the second response in the chain-response pattern; q and r as scope delimiters. Here "before r" means before the first occurrence of r; "after q" means from each occurrence of q onward; "between q and r" means on every segment that starts with q and ends with the next r. | Pattern | Globally | Before r | After q | Between q and r | |---------|----------|------------|-----------|----------------------| | Universality of p | G p | F r -> (p U r) | G(q -> G p) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> (p U r)) | | Absence of p | G ¬p | F r -> (¬p U r) | G(q -> G ¬p) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> (¬p U r)) | | Existence of p | F p | ¬r U (p ∧ ¬r) | G(q -> F p) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> F(p ∧ ¬r)) | | Response p leads to s | G(p -> F s) | G((p ∧ ¬r) -> (¬r U s)) | G(q -> G(p -> F s)) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> G((p ∧ ¬r) -> (¬r U s))) | | Precedence p before s | ¬s W p | F r -> ((¬s ∧ ¬r) W (p ∨ r)) | G(q -> (¬s W p)) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> ((¬s ∧ ¬r) W (p ∨ r))) | | Chain response p leads to s then t | G(p -> F(s ∧ F t)) | G((p ∧ ¬r) -> (¬r U (s ∧ F t))) | G(q -> G(p -> F(s ∧ F t))) | G((q ∧ ¬r ∧ F r) -> G((p ∧ ¬r) -> F(s ∧ F t))) | These formulas are not the only encodings, but they make the scope explicit and are suitable for turning structured English requirements into LTL. Worked Example: Elevator Controller Assume an elevator model with atomic propositions: door_open moving at_f1, at_f2, at_f3 call_f2 overload service_done 6.1 Requirements table | ID | English requirement | LTL formula | Class | Fairness needed? | |----|---------------------|-------------|-------|------------------| | R1 | The elevator must never move while the door is open. | G ¬(moving ∧ door_open) | Safety | No | | R2 | Every call to floor 2 is eventually served. | G(call_f2 -> F at_f2) | Liveness / response | Yes, if scheduler may starve the request forever | | R3 | The elevator can eventually reach floor 3 from startup. | F at_f3 | Reachability | No | | R4 | Once overload is detected, the elevator does not move until overload clears. | G(overload -> (¬moving W ¬overload)) | Safety | No | | R5 | After service completes, the system eventually returns to floor 1. | G(service_done -> F at_f1) | Liveness | Usually yes, if the environment can keep injecting higher-priority calls forever | 6.2 Executable trace fragment | Step | State labels | Interpretation | |------|--------------|----------------| | 0 | {at_f1, door_open} | idle at floor 1 | | 1 | {at_f1, call_f2} | request issued | | 2 | {moving} | doors closed, elevator moving | | 3 | {at_f2, door_open} | request served | Property check: R1 holds on the trace because there is no step with moving ∧ door_open. R2 holds on the trace because the call_f2 at step 1 is followed by at_f2 at step 3. The trace is not a proof of the system-wide property, but it validates that the formalization matches the intended English meaning on one concrete execution. Common Mistakes Calling F goal a liveness proof when only one path to goal has been shown. Reachability is existential; liveness is universal over paths. Forgetting that safety violations have finite bad prefixes and therefore can be checked by reachability. Adding fairness assumptions silently, thereby strengthening the model until the property becomes true. Writing implication-heavy formulas whose antecedent never holds. Confusing G F p with F G p. Practical Checklist [ ] Classify each requirement as safety, liveness, reachability, or a combination before selecting a verification algorithm. [ ] Define the bad states and goal states explicitly. [ ] Check whether any liveness claim depends on a scheduler or environment fairness assumption. [ ] Validate each formalized requirement on one positive and one negative trace. [ ] Look for vacuity, especially when the English requirement begins with "if" or "when." [ ] Keep the English statement next to the LTL formula so the translation can be audited. References Alpern, Bowen, and Fred B. Schneider. 1985. Defining Liveness. *Information Processing Letters* 21 (4): 181-185. <https://doi.org/10.1016/0020-0190(85)90056-0> Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Dwyer, Matthew B., George S. Avrunin, and James C. Corbett. 1999. Patterns in Property Specifications for Finite-State Verification. *ICSE 1999*: 411-420. <https://doi.org/10.1145/302405.302672> Lamport, Leslie. 2002. *Specifying Systems*. Addison-Wesley. <https://lamport.azurewebsites.net/tla/book.html> Manna, Zohar, and Amir Pnueli. 1992. *The Temporal Logic of Reactive and Concurrent Systems*. Springer. Pnueli, Amir. 1977. The Temporal Logic of Programs. *FOCS 1977*: 46-57. <https://doi.org/10.1109/SFCS.1977.32>

documents.title: LTI Systems: Continuous and Discrete
documents.summary: compact matrix form; it models superposition and time invariance, but it does not capture saturation, mode switching, or nonlinear effects unless those are abstracted away. is load-bearing: stability, discretization, and controllability arguments all flow from it. equivalent to every eigenvalue of `A` having strictly negative real part; for discrete-time LTI systems, the corresponding condition is `|λ_i(A_d)| < 1`. discretization is only an approximation and can distort or even destabilize a controller when the sample time is too large. so control-theoretic analysis and formal-methods analysis meet at the mode boundary.
documents.body: LTI Systems: Continuous and Discrete Key Ideas An LTI state-space model captures continuous or sampled linear dynamics in a compact matrix form; it models superposition and time invariance, but it does not capture saturation, mode switching, or nonlinear effects unless those are abstracted away. The continuous-time solution x(t) = e^{At} x(0) + ∫_0^t e^{A(t-τ)} B u(τ) dτ is load-bearing: stability, discretization, and controllability arguments all flow from it. For autonomous continuous-time LTI systems, asymptotic stability is equivalent to every eigenvalue of A having strictly negative real part; for discrete-time LTI systems, the corresponding condition is |λ_i(A_d)| < 1. Exact zero-order-hold discretization preserves the sampled dynamics; Euler discretization is only an approximation and can distort or even destabilize a controller when the sample time is too large. Continuous LTI dynamics often appear as one mode inside a hybrid automaton, so control-theoretic analysis and formal-methods analysis meet at the mode boundary. Intuition Control theory often begins by linearizing the world. Around an operating point, many physical systems behave approximately like "current state times a matrix plus input times a matrix." That approximation is not universally true, but it is powerful because linear algebra gives exact formulas for trajectories, stability, controllability, and observability. The state-space form packages those ideas cleanly. Instead of writing one high order differential equation for each output, we track a vector of internal variables that evolve together. That view is especially useful for autonomous systems, because the controller rarely cares about one scalar alone; it cares about position, velocity, attitude, error states, and actuator commands as a coupled dynamical system. Digital control introduces a second layer. The plant may evolve continuously, but the controller samples sensors and updates commands at discrete times. That means a continuous-time model and its sampled discrete-time implementation are not interchangeable. If the discretization is wrong, the implemented controller may behave very differently from the design on paper. State-Space Representation **Definition (Continuous-time LTI system).** A continuous-time LTI system is a tuple (A, B, C, D) together with the equations ẋ(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) y(t) = C x(t) + D u(t) where: x(t) ∈ R^n is the state vector; u(t) ∈ R^m is the input vector; y(t) ∈ R^p is the output vector; A ∈ R^{n×n}, B ∈ R^{n×m}, C ∈ R^{p×n}, D ∈ R^{p×m} are constant matrices. Gloss: A governs autonomous state evolution. B tells inputs how to push the state. C selects what part of the state is measured as output. D is the direct feedthrough term. Constraint note: Time invariance means the matrices are constant, not functions of t. Minimal reusable example: A = [[0, 1], [0, 0]], B = [[0], [1]], C = [[1, 0]], D = [[0]]. **Definition (Discrete-time LTI system).** A discrete-time LTI system is a tuple (A_d, B_d, C, D) together with the equations x[k+1] = A_d x[k] + B_d u[k] y[k] = C x[k] + D u[k] where the symbols have the analogous meanings in sampled time. Solving the Continuous-Time State Equation We derive the solution for ẋ(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), x(0) = x_0. First consider the homogeneous system ẋ = A x. Define the matrix exponential e^{At} = Σ_{k=0}^∞ (A^k t^k) / k!. It satisfies (d/dt)e^{At} = A e^{At} and e^{A0} = I, so x_h(t) = e^{At} x_0 solves the homogeneous equation. For the forced system, use variation of constants by writing x(t) = e^{At} z(t). Differentiating gives ẋ(t) = A e^{At} z(t) + e^{At} ż(t). Substituting into the state equation and canceling A e^{At} z(t) yields e^{At} ż(t) = B u(t), so ż(t) = e^{-At} B u(t). Integrating from 0 to t, z(t) = x_0 + ∫_0^t e^{-Aτ} B u(τ) dτ. Multiplying by e^{At} gives the full solution: x(t) = e^{At} x_0 + ∫_0^t e^{A(t-τ)} B u(τ) dτ. This formula is the continuous-time analogue of unrolling a recurrence. Stability For the autonomous system ẋ = A x, asymptotic stability means lim_{t->∞} x(t) = 0 for every initial state x_0. **Theorem (Continuous-time eigenvalue test).** The autonomous continuous-time LTI system ẋ = A x is asymptotically stable if and only if every eigenvalue of A satisfies Re(λ_i(A)) < 0. Plain-English reading: every dynamical mode of the matrix must decay exponentially rather than persist or grow. *Proof sketch.* Write the solution as x(t) = e^{At} x_0. In Jordan normal form, e^{At} decomposes into terms of the form t^k e^{λ t}. If every Re(λ) < 0, then the exponential decay dominates any polynomial factor and each term tends to zero, so e^{At} -> 0. Conversely, if some eigenvalue has non-negative real part, then the corresponding modal component does not decay to zero, so there exists an initial condition whose trajectory fails to converge to the origin. Full proofs appear in standard control texts such as Ogata or Kailath. The discrete-time analogue is: **Corollary (Discrete-time eigenvalue test).** The autonomous discrete-time system x[k+1] = A_d x[k] is asymptotically stable if and only if |λ_i(A_d)| < 1 for every eigenvalue of A_d. Exact Discretization and Euler Approximation Suppose the input is held constant over each sample interval of length T_s (zero-order hold). Then the exact sampled model is x[k+1] = A_d x[k] + B_d u[k] with A_d = e^{A T_s} B_d = ∫_0^{T_s} e^{Aτ} B dτ. If A is invertible, the integral simplifies to B_d = A^{-1}(A_d - I) B. Euler discretization replaces the continuous dynamics by a first-order finite difference: x[k+1] ≈ (I + A T_s) x[k] + T_s B u[k]. This is computationally cheap but approximate. **Warning.** The Euler discretization A_d ≈ I + A T_s can destabilize a controller numerically when the sample period is too large or when the continuous-time system is only marginally stable. Exact discretization should be the default for design and analysis. Controllability and Observability **Definition (Controllability).** The discrete-time pair (A_d, B_d) is controllable if the controllability matrix Ctrb = [B_d, A_d B_d, A_d^2 B_d, ..., A_d^{n-1} B_d] has rank n. Interpretation: every state dimension can be influenced by some input sequence. **Definition (Observability).** The pair (A_d, C) is observable if the observability matrix Obs = [C; C A_d; C A_d^2; ...; C A_d^{n-1}] has rank n. Interpretation: the internal state can be reconstructed from output measurements over time. Minimal contrast: Controllable example: the double integrator below has rank([B, AB]) = 2. Uncontrollable example: A = [[1, 0], [0, 1]], B = [[1], [0]] has controllability rank 1, so the second state cannot be actuated. Worked Example: Double Integrator Consider the double-integrator system ẋ(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), with A = [[0, 1], [0, 0]], B = [[0], [1]]. Choose C = [[1, 0]], D = [[0]]. 6.1 Stability and eigenvalues The characteristic polynomial of A is λ^2, so the eigenvalues are λ_1 = 0, λ_2 = 0. Because the real parts are not strictly negative, the continuous-time system is not asymptotically stable. In fact, with u(t) = 0, x_2(t) = x_2(0), x_1(t) = x_1(0) + t x_2(0), so position can drift linearly. 6.2 Exact discretization for T_s = 0.1 s Because A^2 = 0, the matrix exponential truncates: A_d = e^{A T_s} = I + A T_s = [[1, 0.1], [0, 1]]. Also, B_d = ∫_0^{0.1} e^{Aτ} B dτ = [[0.005], [0.1]]. Euler gives the same A_d here only because A^2 = 0; in general it would be an approximation, not an equality. 6.3 Controllability and observability The continuous-time controllability matrix is [B, AB] = [[0, 1], [1, 0]], which has rank 2, so the system is controllable. The observability matrix for C = [[1, 0]] is [C; C A] = [[1, 0], [0, 1]], which also has rank 2, so the state is observable from position measurements over time. 6.4 State-feedback pole placement Let u = -K x with K = [k_1, k_2]. Then A - B K = [[0, 1], [-k_1, -k_2]]. Its characteristic polynomial is λ^2 + k_2 λ + k_1. To place the closed-loop poles at -1 and -2, match to (λ + 1)(λ + 2) = λ^2 + 3 λ + 2, so choose K = [2, 3]. 6.5 Executable sampled trace Use the discrete-time closed-loop model with u[k] = -K x[k], x[0] = [1, 0]^T, and T_s = 0.1. | Step | x[k] | u[k] = -K x[k] | Update | x[k+1] | |------|--------|-------------------|--------|-----------| | 0 | [1, 0]^T | -2.00 | A_d x[0] + B_d u[0] | [0.99, -0.20]^T | | 1 | [0.99, -0.20]^T | -1.38 | A_d x[1] + B_d u[1] | [0.9631, -0.338]^T | | 2 | [0.9631, -0.338]^T | -0.9122 | A_d x[2] + B_d u[2] | [0.92484, -0.42922]^T | The state norm is decreasing, consistent with the chosen stable closed-loop poles. 6.6 Property check The property is "the closed-loop sampled system moves toward the origin." The trace is not a proof by itself, but it is consistent with the eigenvalue test: the continuous-time closed-loop matrix A - B K = [[0, 1], [-2, -3]] has eigenvalues -1 and -2, so the equilibrium is asymptotically stable. Connection Forward In a hybrid automaton, one mode may have flow ẋ = A_i x + B_i u while another mode uses a different pair (A_j, B_j). The LTI analysis here therefore provides the local dynamics inside each mode, while the hybrid model handles switching between them; see 08-hybrid-systems-intuition.md. Common Mistakes Mixing up continuous-time A and discrete-time A_d as if they were the same object. Using Euler discretization in a controller implementation without checking whether the sample time is small enough. Declaring a system stable because all eigenvalues are non-positive; strict negativity of the real parts is required for asymptotic stability. Checking controllability on the wrong pair, such as using the continuous-time rank test on a discretized design without recomputing the matrices. Forgetting that saturation, switching, and dead zones invalidate the linear model outside its operating range. Practical Checklist [ ] Write the full continuous-time and, if implemented digitally, the sampled discrete-time model. [ ] Derive or compute A_d and B_d using exact discretization before using Euler as an approximation. [ ] Check eigenvalues against the correct stability condition for the time domain you are in. [ ] Verify controllability before attempting pole placement. [ ] Verify observability before designing a state estimator. [ ] Validate the model numerically on a small hand-computable trace. References Kailath, Thomas. 1980. *Linear Systems*. Prentice Hall. Lee, Edward A., and Sanjit A. Seshia. 2017. *Introduction to Embedded Systems*. 2nd ed. <https://ptolemy.berkeley.edu/books/leeseshia/> Ogata, Katsuhiko. 2010. *Modern Control Engineering*. 5th ed. Pearson. Rugh, Wilson J. 1996. *Linear System Theory*. 2nd ed. Prentice Hall. Sontag, Eduardo D. 1998. *Mathematical Control Theory*. 2nd ed. Springer. Zhou, Kemin, John C. Doyle, and Keith Glover. 1996. *Robust and Optimal Control*. Prentice Hall.

documents.title: Timed Automata Basics
documents.summary: that all advance uniformly with time; it models deadlines and durations, but not general continuous dynamics such as acceleration or temperature drift. retaining decidable reachability through a finite quotient construction. but only through simple linear constraints and resets. That restriction is what keeps reachability PSPACE-complete rather than undecidable. Difference Bound Matrices (DBMs) make verification practical in tools such as UPPAAL. systems, especially protocols, schedulers, and embedded controllers.
documents.body: Timed Automata Basics Key Ideas A timed automaton extends a finite transition system with real-valued clocks that all advance uniformly with time; it models deadlines and durations, but not general continuous dynamics such as acceleration or temperature drift. Timed automata are strictly more expressive than finite automata while still retaining decidable reachability through a finite quotient construction. The core expressiveness trade-off is this: clocks give dense-time semantics, but only through simple linear constraints and resets. That restriction is what keeps reachability PSPACE-complete rather than undecidable. The region graph proves decidability in theory; zone-based algorithms using Difference Bound Matrices (DBMs) make verification practical in tools such as UPPAAL. Networks of timed automata provide a practical model of concurrent real-time systems, especially protocols, schedulers, and embedded controllers. Intuition A plain finite automaton can say that an acknowledgement eventually follows a request, but it cannot say whether the acknowledgement arrived within 100 milliseconds. For many autonomous and embedded systems, that difference is the entire safety question. Airbags, watchdog timers, mutual-exclusion protocols, and control loops are all sensitive not just to event order but to elapsed time. Timed automata solve this by adding clocks. A clock is a variable that measures how much real time has passed since some event. All clocks increase at the same rate as physical time. A transition may be guarded by a condition such as "take this edge only when x >= 2", and taking that transition may reset some clocks to zero. A location may also carry an invariant such as "x <= 5", meaning the system is not allowed to remain there once five time units have elapsed. The important conceptual point is that this yields a finite description of an infinite-state system. The clock values are real numbers, so there are infinitely many valuations, yet the structure of the allowed comparisons is restricted enough that many valuations behave equivalently for reachability. That is the idea behind the region graph. Formal Definition **Definition (Timed Automaton).** A timed automaton is a tuple A = (Loc, Loc_0, Act, Clk, Inv, E) where: Loc is a finite set of locations; Loc_0 ⊆ Loc is the set of initial locations; Act is a finite set of actions; Clk = {x_1, ..., x_n} is a finite set of clocks; Inv : Loc -> Φ(Clk) assigns an invariant to each location; E ⊆ Loc × Act × Φ(Clk) × 2^Clk × Loc is the set of edges. Each edge e = (ℓ, a, g, r, ℓ') consists of: source location ℓ; action a; guard g, a clock constraint that must hold to fire the edge; reset set r ⊆ Clk, the clocks reset to zero; target location ℓ'. Gloss: Locations are the discrete modes. Clocks store elapsed time since relevant events. Guards enable action transitions only when timing constraints hold. Invariants bound how long time may elapse in a location. Resets restart timing measurements. Constraint note: The semantics only allows time to elapse while the invariant remains true. Guard and invariant satisfiability must be checked together; otherwise a model can contain syntactically present but semantically impossible transitions. Minimal reusable example: Loc = {Idle, Wait, Alarm} Loc_0 = {Idle} Act = {request, timeout} Clk = {x} Inv(Wait) = (x <= 3), all other invariants are true edges: (Idle, request, true, {x}, Wait) and (Wait, timeout, x >= 3, ∅, Alarm). Clock Constraints and Semantics Clock constraints are generated by: atomic constraints x ~ c or x - y ~ c, where x, y ∈ Clk, c ∈ Z, and ~ ∈ {<, <=, =, >=, >}. A clock valuation is a function ν : Clk -> R_{>=0}. It satisfies a guard or invariant in the obvious way, for example: ν ⊨ x <= 3 iff ν(x) <= 3; ν ⊨ x - y < 2 iff ν(x) - ν(y) < 2. For d ∈ R_{>=0}, define ν + d by (ν + d)(x) = ν(x) + d. For a reset set r ⊆ Clk, define ν[r := 0] by resetting every clock in r to zero and leaving the others unchanged. The semantic states of a timed automaton are pairs (ℓ, ν) with ℓ ∈ Loc and ν ⊨ Inv(ℓ). There are two kinds of transitions: **Delay transition:** (ℓ, ν) --d--> (ℓ, ν + d) for d ∈ R_{>=0} iff for all 0 <= d' <= d, ν + d' ⊨ Inv(ℓ). **Action transition:** (ℓ, ν) --a--> (ℓ', ν[r := 0]) iff there exists an edge (ℓ, a, g, r, ℓ') ∈ E such that ν ⊨ g and ν[r := 0] ⊨ Inv(ℓ'). Thus a timed automaton induces a timed transition system with infinitely many states because the valuation component ranges over reals. Region Graph and Decidability Two clock valuations are region-equivalent when they agree on the integer parts of clocks up to the maximum constant c_max, on which clocks have zero fractional part, and on the ordering of the fractional parts. Alur and Dill showed that only finitely many such regions exist. The quotient of the semantic state space by this equivalence is the **region graph**. Reachability in the timed automaton reduces to reachability in this finite graph. The size of the region graph is bounded by O(|Loc| * |Act| * (2|Clk|)! * (2*c_max + 2)^|Clk|). That bound is large, which is why explicit region construction is mainly a proof device rather than the implementation strategy of practical tools. **Theorem (Alur-Dill reachability).** Reachability for timed automata is decidable and PSPACE-complete. Plain-English reading: despite the infinite real-valued state space, reachability can still be decided algorithmically, but the worst-case problem is expensive. *Proof sketch.* The region equivalence partitions the infinite valuation space into finitely many classes that preserve the truth of guards, invariants, and future reachability. Construct the finite region graph and run ordinary graph search on it. PSPACE-hardness follows by reduction from linear-space automata; PSPACE membership follows because the search can be performed without storing the full graph explicitly. Full proof in Alur and Dill 1994. Zone-Based Algorithms and DBMs Practical tools such as UPPAAL do not enumerate regions. They represent convex sets of valuations called **zones**: conjunctions of bounds x - y <= c; stored efficiently as a Difference Bound Matrix. A DBM supports: delay closure; guard intersection; clock reset; emptiness checking; canonical normalization. Zone algorithms are typically much faster in practice because they manipulate whole symbolic sets of valuations at once instead of single regions. Networks of Timed Automata Real systems are often modeled as parallel compositions of smaller automata. Each component has local locations and clocks. Components synchronize on shared action labels, producing a global configuration whose location is a tuple. Composition is attractive because it matches modular design, but it also creates state explosion: even if each component is small, the product of their locations and symbolic clock zones can grow rapidly. Worked Example: Timed Mutual Exclusion We model two processes competing for a critical section and a separate lock automaton mediating access. 6.1 Component automata For each process P_i with i ∈ {1, 2}: Loc_i = {Idle_i, Wait_i, Crit_i} Loc_{0,i} = {Idle_i} Act_i = {req_i, enter_i, exit_i} Clk_i = {x_i} Inv(Wait_i) = (x_i <= 2) edges: (Idle_i, req_i, true, {x_i}, Wait_i) (Wait_i, enter_i, x_i >= 1, ∅, Crit_i) (Crit_i, exit_i, true, ∅, Idle_i) The lock automaton L has no clocks: Loc_L = {Free, Reserved1, Held1, Reserved2, Held2} Loc_{0,L} = {Free} actions req_1, enter_1, exit_1, req_2, enter_2, exit_2 edges: Free --req_1--> Reserved1 --enter_1--> Held1 --exit_1--> Free Free --req_2--> Reserved2 --enter_2--> Held2 --exit_2--> Free 6.2 Reachable region graph fragment Because the lock has only one free token, the reachable global quotient states fall into a small pattern: | Region state | Composite location | Clock region | Meaning | |--------------|--------------------|--------------|---------| | q0 | (Idle_1, Idle_2, Free) | x_1 = x_2 = 0 | initial | | q1 | (Wait_1, Idle_2, Reserved1) | 0 <= x_1 < 1 | process 1 waiting | | q2 | (Crit_1, Idle_2, Held1) | clocks irrelevant | process 1 in critical section | | q3 | (Idle_1, Wait_2, Reserved2) | 0 <= x_2 < 1 | process 2 waiting | | q4 | (Idle_1, Crit_2, Held2) | clocks irrelevant | process 2 in critical section | There is no reachable region whose composite location contains both Crit_1 and Crit_2, because the lock automaton has no location that simultaneously permits Held1 and Held2. 6.3 Executable trace | Step | Location tuple | Clock vals | Type | Guard / invariant check | Action / delay | |------|----------------|------------|------|-------------------------|----------------| | 0 | (Idle_1, Idle_2, Free) | x_1 = 0, x_2 = 0 | initial | all invariants true | start | | 1 | (Wait_1, Idle_2, Reserved1) | x_1 = 0, x_2 = 0 | action | req_1 synchronizes with lock | req_1 | | 2 | (Wait_1, Idle_2, Reserved1) | x_1 = 1.2, x_2 = 1.2 | delay | x_1 <= 2 holds | d = 1.2 | | 3 | (Crit_1, Idle_2, Held1) | x_1 = 1.2, x_2 = 1.2 | action | guard x_1 >= 1 holds | enter_1 | | 4 | (Idle_1, Idle_2, Free) | x_1 = 1.2, x_2 = 1.2 | action | exit enabled | exit_1 | | 5 | (Idle_1, Wait_2, Reserved2) | x_1 = 1.2, x_2 = 0 | action | req_2 synchronizes with lock | req_2 | 6.4 Property check The mutual-exclusion property is A[] not (P1.Crit and P2.Crit). The trace satisfies it, and the reachable region graph fragment explains why: the lock's location is either Free, Reserved1, Held1, Reserved2, or Held2. None of those admits simultaneous ownership by both processes. UPPAAL and Property Queries UPPAAL models timed automata as: templates with locations, invariants, and edges; channels for synchronization; integer variables and clocks; TCTL-like queries. Common query forms: A[] p: on all reachable states, p holds. E<> p: there exists a path reaching a state where p holds. A<> p: on all paths, p is eventually reached. p --> q: whenever p occurs, q eventually follows. Interpretation matters: a satisfied A[] query is a global invariant over the model; a failed query produces a diagnostic trace; a query only proves something about the model, not about omitted dynamics. Deadline requirements A requirement such as "within T time units of event A, event B must occur" is often handled by an observer clock: reset clock x when A occurs; move to an error location if x > T before B; verify A[] not Error. Common Mistakes Modeling general continuous dynamics with timed automata. Clocks always have derivative 1; they are not arbitrary state variables. Forgetting invariants, thereby allowing time to diverge in a waiting state that was meant to enforce a deadline. Reading a zone-based diagnostic trace as if it were a unique concrete run; symbolic traces may represent many valuations. Building a network whose synchronization labels do not match, silently disconnecting components. Ignoring Zeno-like urgent cycles created by zero-time transitions. Practical Checklist [ ] List every clock and the event whose elapsed time it measures. [ ] Check guards and invariants together for satisfiability. [ ] Confirm that deadline properties are encoded by observers or queries that actually mention time. [ ] Use symbolic zones or DBMs in practice rather than explicit region construction. [ ] Inspect whether composition introduces urgent cycles or deadlocks. [ ] Validate the model on at least one accepted and one rejected timed trace. References Alur, Rajeev, and David L. Dill. 1994. A Theory of Timed Automata. *Theoretical Computer Science* 126 (2): 183-235. <https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-3975(94)90010-8> Baier, Christel, and Joost-Pieter Katoen. 2008. *Principles of Model Checking*. MIT Press. <https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262026499/principles-of-model-checking/> Behrmann, Gerd, Alexandre David, and Kim G. Larsen. 2004. A Tutorial on UPPAAL. *Formal Methods for the Design of Real-Time Systems*. <https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-30080-9_7> Larsen, Kim G., Paul Pettersson, and Wang Yi. 1997. UPPAAL in a Nutshell. *STTT* 1: 134-152. <https://doi.org/10.1007/s100090050010> Tripakis, Stavros. 1998. Verification of Timed Systems. *CAV 1998 Tutorial Notes*. UPPAAL tool suite. <https://uppaal.org/>
